title: Psalms 117
subtitle: Praise the LORD, All You Nations

heb.paragraph: "Praise the LORD, all you nations (gōyîm); extol him, all you peoples" (v.1). The shortest psalm — two verses. The shortest chapter in the Bible. Paul quotes this in Romans 15:11 for Gentile inclusion. The command goes to ALL nations, ALL peoples. The scope is universal.

notes.note: "Praise the LORD, all you nations." The universal summons in two verses. Paul uses this in Romans 15:11 as proof that the Gentiles were always meant to praise God. MacArthur emphasizes the doctrinal significance of this passage within the framework of systematic theology. The text establishes principles that remain normative for the church — not merely descriptive of ancient Israel but prescriptive for how God's people should understand His character and respond in obedience.

refs.note: Paul quotes this psalm to show God's plan always included Gentile worship.
The great multitude from every nation fulfills the universal praise envisioned here.
Gentiles brought near through Christ join Israel in praising the LORD's faithfulness.

hist.context: Psalm 117 is the shortest chapter in the Bible—just two verses—yet carries immense theological weight. Its summons to all nations anticipates the Gentile inclusion that dominates NT theology. The psalm concludes the Egyptian Hallel's opening movement and was sung at Passover, Pentecost, and Tabernacles. Its brevity belies its scope: all peoples, all nations, praising Israel's God for covenant faithfulness.

heb.paragraph: "For great is his love (ḥesed) toward us, and the faithfulness of the LORD endures forever. Praise the LORD" (v.2). Two reasons for universal praise: great ḥesed and enduring faithfulness. Two verses, two reasons, one hallelujah. The shortest psalm carries the widest scope.

notes.note: "Great is his love toward us, and the faithfulness of the LORD endures forever." Two attributes: love (great in degree) and faithfulness (endless in duration). Hallelujah. MacArthur emphasizes the doctrinal significance of this passage within the framework of systematic theology. The text establishes principles that remain normative for the church — not merely descriptive of ancient Israel but prescriptive for how God's people should understand His character and respond in obedience.

refs.note: The LORD's faithfulness endures forever—a refrain echoed across the Psalter.
God's steadfast love and faithfulness are new every morning.
God's faithfulness grounds his forgiveness of sins.

hist.context: The psalm's two keywords—hesed (steadfast love) and emet (faithfulness/truth)—form the core covenant vocabulary of the Hebrew Bible. These attributes define YHWH's character in Exodus 34:6. The closing 'Hallelujah' (Praise the LORD) prepares for Psalm 118's climactic celebration. Together, 117's brevity and universality make it a hinge between the Egyptian Hallel's personal testimonies and its triumphant conclusion.

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: Paul uses this in Romans 15:11 as proof that the Gentiles were always meant to praise God…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVFor great is his love toward us, and the faithfulness of the Lord endures forever. Praise the Lord.ESVFor great is his steadfast love toward us, and the faithfulness of the LORD endures forever. Praise the LORD!NIVPraise the Lord, all you nations; extol him, all you peoples.ESVPraise the LORD, all nations! Extol him, all peoples!

rec.text: ""Praise the LORD, all you nations." The universal summons in two verses. Paul uses this in Romans 15:11 as proof that the Gentiles were always meant to praise God."

rows.label: Ḥesed Forever — 117:2
rows.text: "Great is his LOVE. Faithfulness endures FOREVER." Two verses, two reasons, one hallelujah.

hebtext.note: "Praise the LORD, all you nations (gōyîm); extol him, all you peoples" (v.1). The shortest psalm — two verses. The shortest chapter in the Bible. Paul quotes this in Romans 15:11 for Gentile inclusion. The command goes to ALL nations, ALL peoples. The scope is universal.

"For great is his love (ḥesed) toward us, and the faithfulness of the LORD endures forever. Praise the LORD" (v.2). Two reasons for universal praise: great ḥesed and enduring faithfulness. Two verses, two reasons, one hallelujah. The shortest psalm carries the widest scope.

themes.note: Mission peaks at 10: the shortest chapter in the Bible commands ALL nations to praise. Prophecy: Paul quotes for Gentile inclusion (Rom 15:11). The universal psalm.

prayer_prompt: The shortest psalm calls all nations to praise the LORD for His great love. Consider asking God about His love that reaches beyond your tribe — what nations He loves that you've ignored.
